edges.description: Immune-mediated hepatocellular injury during acute HCV infection releases intracellular transaminases, raising serum ALT/AST.
intermediate_mechanisms: Immune-mediated hepatocyte injury with leakage of cytosolic transaminases

edges.description: Hepatocellular injury in symptomatic acute hepatitis C impairs bilirubin uptake, conjugation, and excretion, producing jaundice.
intermediate_mechanisms: Hepatocellular dysfunction with impaired bilirubin handling and cholestasis

edges.description: Acute hepatitic inflammation produces constitutional gastrointestinal symptoms including nausea in symptomatic infection.
Suppressed interferon induction reduces the host antiviral response that can otherwise clear acute HCV.
Persistence of viremia beyond six months defines chronic infection.

nodes.description: Persistence of HCV viremia for more than six months after recent acquisition represents progression from acute to chronic hepatitis C virus infection.

evidence.reference_title: Acute Hepatitis C: Current Status and Future Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: approximately 50-70% of individuals with recently acquired hepatitis C will develop a chronic infection, defined as the persistence of viremia for a period exceeding six months.

nodes.description: Current practice favors early direct-acting antiviral treatment for confirmed acute HCV rather than waiting for spontaneous clearance.

evidence.reference_title: Acute hepatitis C virus infection: clinical update and remaining challenges.
evidence.snippet: DAAs should be initiated early in acute HCV infection prior to spontaneous viral clearance.

nodes.description: Blood-borne HCV exposure establishes early viremia and infection of the liver; during this recent-infection phase, outcomes diverge toward spontaneous clearance or chronic viremia.

evidence.reference_title: Acute Hepatitis C: Current Status and Future Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: The early phase of HCV infection represents a pivotal point in the evolution of hepatitis C.

nodes.description: Acute hepatocellular injury may be reflected by elevated serum transaminases.

evidence.reference_title: Acute/recent HCV infection. Clinical course, viral replication kunetic and disease outcome.

nodes.description: HCV NS3/4A protease cleaves and inactivates host MAVS and TRIF adaptor proteins, suppressing sensory pathways that induce interferons after viral pathogen-associated molecular pattern recognition. This blunts innate antiviral signaling and promotes viral persistence after acute infection.

biological_processes: symbiont-mediated suppression of host type I interferon-mediated signaling pathway

evidence.reference_title: Innate and adaptive immune responses in HCV infections.
evidence.snippet: the HCV NS3/4A protease can efficiently cleave and inactivate two important signalling molecules in the sensory pathways that react to HCV pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) to induce IFNs, i.e., the mitochondrial anti-viral signalling protein (MAVS) and the Toll-IL-1 receptor-domain-containing adaptor-inducing IFN-β (TRIF).

nodes.description: Host antiviral immunity can clear HCV, but many recently acquired infections persist beyond six months and become chronic HCV infection.

evidence.reference_title: Acute Hepatitis C: Current Status and Future Perspectives.
evidence.snippet: approximately 50-70% of individuals with recently acquired hepatitis C will develop a chronic infection, defined as the persistence of viremia for a period exceeding six months.

nodes.description: Jaundice can occur in symptomatic acute hepatitis C.

evidence.reference_title: Acute/recent HCV infection. Clinical course, viral replication kunetic and disease outcome.
evidence.snippet: Out of 4 symptomatics 3 were with jaundice.

nodes.description: Nausea is a compatible symptom in symptomatic acute viral hepatitis.
